texto_por_actor.Arab Republic of Egypt: Water flow patterns to Egypt become less predictable
texto_por_actor.Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia: Downstream water flow becomes more variable and unpredictable
texto_por_actor.Republic of Sudan: Sudan faces increased uncertainty about downstream water flows

Arab Republic of Egypt.texto: Egypt demands a legally binding agreement (not voluntary guidelines) that obliges Ethiopia to release guaranteed minimum flows during drought years to prevent irreversible damage. Invokes the international principle of 'no significant harm' and its historical rights based on the 1929 and 1959 treaties. Declares that all options remain open to protect its vital resource.
International mediators face increased pressure to propose compromise frameworks
Mediators prioritize water-sharing formulas over electricity generation schedules
Arab Republic of Egypt gains negotiating advantage through aligned mediator priorities
Ethiopia perceives increased international pressure against its development priorities
Ethiopia accelerates unilateral dam operations to establish facts
Water flow patterns to Egypt become less predictable

Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia.texto: Ethiopia invokes the principle of 'Equitable and Reasonable Utilization' recognized in customary international law, rejecting the 1929 and 1959 treaties as valid legal basis. Proposes flexible and revisable operational guidelines that do not restrict its future development rights, arguing that a rigid treaty would perpetuate historical inequities. Maintains that flow regulation will benefit the region by stabilizing discharge and reducing floods.
Ethiopia retains unilateral control over GERD operations
Ethiopia can respond to domestic energy demands without external approval
Ethiopia can adjust GERD water releases based on electricity demand
Ethiopia can reduce water releases during low electricity demand periods
Downstream water flow becomes more variable and unpredictable

Republic of Sudan.texto: Sudan conditions its acceptance on the implementation of a robust real-time technical data exchange mechanism (daily/hourly). While acknowledging that the GERD can control seasonal floods affecting Khartoum and reduce sedimentation at Roseires, it considers unacceptable the operational risk of a 74 billion cubic meter dam upstream without binding coordination, fearing the collapse of its own irrigation and electricity generation systems.
Sudan demands legally binding coordination protocols before dam operations
Sudan refuses to participate in current GERD operational discussions
Sudan faces increased uncertainty about downstream water flows

Egypt seeks alternative downstream allies beyond Sudan

Proposes flexible and revisable operational guidelines that do not restrict its future development rights, arguing that a rigid treaty would perpetuate historical inequities. Maintains that flow regulation will benefit the region by stabilizing discharge and reducing floods.
Egypt rejects Ethiopia's legal framework as illegitimate
Ethiopia views negotiations as fundamentally deadlocked on legal principles
Ethiopia seeks alternative diplomatic frameworks outside trilateral negotiations

While acknowledging that the GERD can control seasonal floods affecting Khartoum and reduce sedimentation at Roseires, it considers unacceptable the operational risk of a 74 billion cubic meter dam upstream without binding coordination, fearing the collapse of its own irrigation and electricity generation systems.
Sudan views GERD as existential infrastructure threat
Sudan seeks alternative water security partnerships
Sudan explores Red Sea desalination project partnerships
Sudan reduces dependence on Nile water allocation negotiations
Sudan shifts diplomatic focus toward non-Nile regional partnerships
